name: Arindam Chakrabarti
dates_notes: Living scholar. Birth year is commonly given as 1957 but could not be confirmed from Wikipedia, Stony Brook, Ashoka, or other primary biographical records consulted (2026-06-15); his B.A. (Calcutta, 1976) and M.A. (Calcutta, 1978) imply a mid-to-late-1950s birth. dates_tier is contested rather than confirmed-from-records.

dates_evidence.description: Public institutional and bibliographic records: B.A. (1976) and M.A. (1978) from the University of Calcutta; DPhil, Oxford (1982), supervised by Sir Peter Strawson and Sir Michael Dummett. Taught at the University of Calcutta, University College London, the University of Washington, the University of Delhi, and for ca. 26 years at the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa (founding director of the EPOCH cross-cultural philosophy project); subsequently Mattoo Endowed Chair in Indic Humanities, Department of Philosophy, Stony Brook University (from 2018), and later Ashoka University.

core_thesis: Arindam Chakrabarti took his D.Phil. at Oxford in 1982 under P. F. Strawson and Michael Dummett, and has taught at the University of Calcutta, University College London, the University of Delhi, the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa (Lenney Professor of Indian Philosophy of Mind, 1997–2018), and Stony Brook University, before joining Ashoka University. His project is a “fusion philosophy” that reads classical Indian (especially Nyāya and Kashmir Śaiva), analytic, and continental philosophy together — programmatically stated in *Comparative Philosophy without Borders* (ed. with Ralph Weber, 2015) — and he carries the engagement into Sanskrit itself, having composed an introduction to twentieth-century Western epistemology in Sanskrit and worked on a Sanskrit book on Western philosophy; he was awarded the traditional title *Mahāmahopādhyāya* in 2019. He also writes in Bengali. Chakrabarti argues for a linked realism in which external objects, enduring subjects, and other subjects stand or fall together. In *Realisms Interlinked*, cross-sensory re-identification supplies the opening case: the object touched and the object seen must be one object, and that act of re-identification also requires a subject that owns and compares experiences. Against Buddhist, Humean, Parfitian, and fictionalist reductions, he uses Nyāya and analytic arguments to defend a substantial self without collapsing that self into introspected mental episodes or bodily processes (Chakrabarti, *Realisms Interlinked*, Introduction; chs. 1, 13, 16). The same framework extends to mind and language. The inner sense, or *manas*, mediates attention, introspection, cross-modal comparison, and imaginative perception; testimony and teaching are genuine sources of understanding because objectivity is public and intersubjective, not sealed within a solitary first person. Knowledge of other minds is therefore difficult but indispensable: the “you” is distinct from the “I,” yet communication, emotion, and shared reference require real other subjects (Chakrabarti, *Realisms Interlinked*, chs. 13, 17, 20). In his Nyāya epistemology he argues against indeterminate (*nirvikalpaka*) perception, holding that perceptual awareness is already qualified and articulable rather than a bare immaculate given (Chakrabarti, “Against Immaculate Perception: Seven Reasons for Eliminating Nirvikalpaka Perception from Nyāya,” *Philosophy East and West* 50.1, 2000).

engaged_works.title: Denying Existence: The Logic, Epistemology and Pragmatics of Negative Existentials and Fictional Discourse
engaged_works.title_iast: Denying Existence: The Logic, Epistemology and Pragmatics of Negative Existentials and Fictional Discourse

ascription_evidence.description: Standard bibliographic record; the title appears in the Synthese Library series catalogue.

engaged_works.summary: A monograph on the long-standing logical problem of how one can meaningfully and truly deny the existence of something — the puzzle of singular negative existentials ("Pegasus does not exist") and of reference to and discourse about fictional and non-existent objects. Chakrabarti works the problem across both Western analytic philosophy (Russell, Frege, Strawson, the descriptivist and direct-reference traditions on reference and presupposition) and classical Indian philosophy of language and epistemology (the Nyāya and Navya-Nyāya treatment of *abhāva*, absence, and of cognition of the non-existent). The book develops an account integrating the logic, epistemology, and pragmatics of negative existentials, and is one of his earliest sustained demonstrations that Nyāya analysis can be brought to bear on a problem at the center of analytic philosophy of language.

engaged_works.title: Realisms Interlinked: Objects, Subjects, and Other Subjects
engaged_works.title_iast: Realisms Interlinked: Objects, Subjects, and Other Subjects

ascription_evidence.description: Standard bibliographic record; widely reviewed in comparative and analytic philosophy.

engaged_works.summary: A large monograph drawing together over twenty-five years of Chakrabarti's research in epistemology, metaphysics, and philosophy of mind, organized around three interlinked concepts: objects in the world, the self who perceives them, and other subjects (other minds). The book argues for an interlinked set of realisms — realism about external objects, about the perceiving self, and about other subjects — defending each against idealist and eliminativist alternatives. It engages topics including the refutation of idealism, the definability of truth, the existence of non-conceptual perception, and our knowledge of other minds, working classical Indian (especially Nyāya) and modern Western analytic resources together rather than treating them as separate traditions. The book is the principal synthetic statement of Chakrabarti's cross-cultural analytic metaphysics and epistemology.

engaged_works.title: Knowing from Words: Western and Indian Philosophical Analysis of Understanding and Testimony
engaged_works.title_iast: Knowing from Words: Western and Indian Philosophical Analysis of Understanding and Testimony
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Co-edited with B. K. Matilal; published in the Synthese Library by Kluwer Academic (records give 1993/1994).
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Co-edited by Bimal Krishna Matilal and Arindam Chakrabarti (Kluwer / Synthese Library).

ascription_evidence.description: Standard bibliographic record; foundational collection on the epistemology of testimony in comparative philosophy.

engaged_works.summary: A co-edited collection (with B. K. Matilal) on the epistemology of testimony — how we come to know things from the words of others — bringing Western analytic epistemology of testimony into dialogue with the Indian (especially Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā) treatment of *śabda-pramāṇa*, verbal testimony as an independent source of knowledge. The volume gathers essays on understanding, meaning, and the warrant of testimonial belief across the two traditions, and is one of the founding works of the comparative epistemology of testimony.

engaged_works.title: Comparative Philosophy without Borders
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Co-edited with Ralph Weber; published 2015, Bloomsbury (Continuum).

ascription_evidence.description: Standard bibliographic record for the edited volume.

engaged_works.summary: A co-edited programmatic collection arguing for a practice of comparative philosophy that reasons across traditions to address philosophical problems directly, rather than merely cataloguing similarities and differences between bounded traditions. The editors' framing — "without borders" — calls for treating Indian, Chinese, and Western sources as co-participants in a single philosophical inquiry. The volume articulates the methodological position underlying Chakrabarti's own cross-cultural work in epistemology and metaphysics.

engaged_works.title: The Bloomsbury Research Handbook of Indian Aesthetics and the Philosophy of Art
engaged_works.title_iast: The Bloomsbury Research Handbook of Indian Aesthetics and the Philosophy of Art
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Edited volume; published by Bloomsbury (records give 2016/2017).

ascription_evidence.description: Standard bibliographic record for the edited handbook.

engaged_works.summary: An edited research handbook on Indian aesthetics and the philosophy of art, gathering essays on classical Indian theories of *rasa*, aesthetic emotion, the ontology of the artwork, and the relation between aesthetic and philosophical experience, set in dialogue with Western philosophy of art. As editor, Chakrabarti frames Indian aesthetics as a body of philosophical theory worth engaging on its own argumentative terms; the handbook extends his cross-cultural method into the domain of aesthetics.

reception_notes: Read within analytic, comparative, and classical-Indian philosophy; founding director of the EPOCH cross-cultural philosophy project at the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa.
